content.text: In Microsoft Entra ID a user object represents an identity that can sign in and access resources. Users come in two types — Members (internal) and Guests (external B2B invitees) — and understanding the differences in their permissions, directory access, and licensing implications is foundational to designing identity architectures.

attrs.alt: Entra ID user types: Member, Guest (B2B), and non-human identities

content.text: Every user object in Entra ID has a userType property that is either Member or Guest. This controls default directory permissions and licensing behaviour.
Against home tenant (their own Entra ID or social IdP)
Restricted (cannot enumerate other users by default)
Limited — cannot be Global Admin by default
1:5 MAU ratio (5 free guests per paid member user)
Yes — invitation or self-service sign-up flow
Default guest restrictions mean guests cannot enumerate the full directory. This is by design — tighten it further or loosen it per your collaboration needs via External Collaboration Settings.

import requests, secrets, string

def generate_temp_password(length: int = 16) -> str:
    chars = string.ascii_letters + string.digits + "!@#$%"
    return ''.join(secrets.choice(chars) for _ in range(length))

new_user = {
    "displayName": "Alice Smith",
    "userPrincipalName": "alice@contoso.com",
    "mailNickname": "alice",
    "accountEnabled": True,
    "passwordProfile": {
        "forceChangePasswordNextSignIn": True,
        "password": generate_temp_password()
    },
    "jobTitle": "Software Engineer",
    "department": "Engineering",
    "usageLocation": "US",           # required before license assignment
    "employeeType": "Employee",
    "employeeHireDate": "2026-08-01T00:00:00Z"
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=new_user
)
resp.raise_for_status()
user = resp.json()
print("Created user:", user["id"], user["userPrincipalName"])

import csv, requests, secrets, string

def create_user(token: str, row: dict) -> dict:
    body = {
        "displayName": f"{row['FirstName']} {row['LastName']}",
        "userPrincipalName": row["UPN"],
        "mailNickname": row["UPN"].split("@")[0],
        "accountEnabled": True,
        "passwordProfile": {
            "forceChangePasswordNextSignIn": True,
            "password": ''.join(secrets.choice(string.ascii_letters + string.digits + "!@#") for _ in range(16))
        },
        "department": row["Department"],
        "jobTitle": row["JobTitle"],
        "usageLocation": row.get("Country", "US")
    }
    r = requests.post(
        "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
        json=body
    )
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()

# users.csv: FirstName,LastName,UPN,Department,JobTitle,Country
with open("users.csv") as f:
    for row in csv.DictReader(f):
        u = create_user(access_token, row)
        print(f"Created: {u['userPrincipalName']}  id={u['id']}")
Creating a User via Azure CLI

# Create a member user
az ad user create     --display-name "Bob Jones"     --user-principal-name bob@contoso.com     --password "TempP@ss2026!"     --force-change-password-next-sign-in true     --mail-nickname bob

# Get the user's object ID
az ad user show --id bob@contoso.com --query id -o tsv

# Create using the PowerShell Az module
New-MgUser -DisplayName "Carol Lee" `
    -UserPrincipalName "carol@contoso.com" `
    -MailNickname "carol" `
    -AccountEnabled `
    -PasswordProfile @{Password="TempP@ss!"; ForceChangePasswordNextSignIn=$true} `
    -UsageLocation "US"

Guest users are invited rather than created. An invitation email is sent to the external email address. The guest must redeem the invitation before their account becomes active. Redemption authenticates the user against their home identity provider (their own Entra ID, Google, Microsoft account, or one-time passcode via email).

import requests

invitation = {
    "invitedUserEmailAddress": "partner@fabrikam.com",
    "invitedUserDisplayName": "Partner User",
    "inviteRedirectUrl": "https://myapp.contoso.com",
    "sendInvitationMessage": True,
    "invitedUserMessageInfo": {
        "customizedMessageBody": "Hi! You've been invited to access Contoso resources. Please accept below.",
        "ccRecipients": [{"emailAddress": {"address": "admin@contoso.com"}}]
    }
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/invitations",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=invitation
)
data = resp.json()
print("Guest UPN:", data["invitedUser"]["userPrincipalName"])
print("Invite URL:", data["inviteRedeemUrl"])   # share this if email doesn't arrive

import csv, requests

def invite_guest(token: str, email: str, display_name: str, redirect_url: str) -> dict:
    resp = requests.post(
        "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/invitations",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
        json={
            "invitedUserEmailAddress": email,
            "invitedUserDisplayName": display_name,
            "inviteRedirectUrl": redirect_url,
            "sendInvitationMessage": True
        }
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

# guests.csv: Email,DisplayName
REDIRECT = "https://myportal.contoso.com"
with open("guests.csv") as f:
    for row in csv.DictReader(f):
        result = invite_guest(access_token, row["Email"], row["DisplayName"], REDIRECT)
        status = result.get("status", "unknown")
        print(f"{row['Email']:<40} Status: {status}")

Entra ID user objects have dozens of standard properties. A subset are indexed (searchable), while others are read/write only by administrators. Extension attributes allow you to add custom fields without modifying the Entra ID schema.
Immutable object ID — use as stable identifier in code, not UPN
Sign-in name; must be unique in tenant; can be changed
Primary email; may differ from UPN
False = account disabled; cannot sign in
True = synced from AD DS via Entra Connect

import requests

# Read specific user properties
user = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/alice@contoso.com",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
    params={"$select": "id,displayName,userPrincipalName,accountEnabled,department,signInActivity"}
).json()

print(f"ID: {user['id']}")
print(f"Name: {user['displayName']}")
print(f"Enabled: {user['accountEnabled']}")
last_signin = user.get("signInActivity", {}).get("lastSignInDateTime", "Never")
print(f"Last sign-in: {last_signin}")

# Update properties
requests.patch(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{user['id']}",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={
        "jobTitle": "Senior Software Engineer",
        "department": "Platform Engineering",
        "officeLocation": "Seattle",
        "businessPhones": ["+1 206 555 0100"]
    }
)

# Disable an account (leaver scenario)
requests.patch(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{user['id']}",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={"accountEnabled": False}
)

A user object goes through predictable lifecycle stages: created (joiner), possibly transferred (mover), then disabled and eventually deleted (leaver). Entra ID soft-deletes users — a deleted user stays in the recycle bin for 30 days and can be restored.
Finding Stale Accounts (No Sign-In in 90+ Days)

import requests, secrets, string

def reset_password(token: str, user_id: str, force_change: bool = True) -> str:
    temp_pwd = ''.join(secrets.choice(string.ascii_letters + string.digits + "!@#$") for _ in range(16))
    requests.patch(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{user_id}",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
        json={"passwordProfile": {"password": temp_pwd, "forceChangePasswordNextSignIn": force_change}}
    ).raise_for_status()
    return temp_pwd

temp = reset_password(access_token, "<user-id>")
print(f"Temporary password: {temp}")  # communicate securely to the user
Use object ID, not UPN, as a stable key in code
UPNs can change (surname change, domain rename); the id GUID never changes
Always set usageLocation before assigning licenses
License assignment fails silently if usageLocation is blank
accountEnabled=false blocks new logins but doesn't terminate existing tokens; call revokeSignInSessions explicitly
Guests accumulate — run Access Reviews on guest user groups every 90 days
Never store UPN in external databases as a join key
Use immutable object id or onPremisesImmutableId (for synced users)
Synced users cannot have cloud passwords reset unless writeback is enabled
Enable password writeback in Entra Connect before offering SSPR to hybrid users
Extension Attributes and Custom User Properties

Standard user properties cover most scenarios, but you sometimes need org-specific fields — employee ID, cost centre, badge number, or clearance level. Entra ID supports two mechanisms: the 15 on-premises extension attributes (onPremisesExtensionAttributes) synced from AD DS, and open extensions or schema extensions for cloud-native custom attributes.

import requests

# AD DS extensionAttribute1–15 are synced via Entra Connect
# Read them on a synced user
user = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/alice@contoso.com",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
    params={"$select": "onPremisesExtensionAttributes"}
).json()

ext = user.get("onPremisesExtensionAttributes", {})
print("employeeId:", ext.get("extensionAttribute1"))   # mapped in AD DS
print("costCenter:", ext.get("extensionAttribute2"))
print("badgeNumber:", ext.get("extensionAttribute3"))

# These are read-only in Entra ID when onPremisesSyncEnabled=True
# Update them in AD DS, wait for sync cycle (~30 min), then read again

import requests

USER_ID = "<user-object-id>"
EXT_NAME = "com.contoso.hrData"

# Write a custom extension to a user object
resp = requests.post(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{USER_ID}/extensions",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={
        "@odata.type": "microsoft.graph.openTypeExtension",
        "extensionName": EXT_NAME,
        "employeeId": "EMP-20241234",
        "costCenter": "CC-4420",
        "clearanceLevel": "Confidential",
        "reviewDate": "2027-01-01"
    }
)
print(resp.status_code)  # 201 Created

# Read back the extension
user_with_ext = requests.get(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{USER_ID}",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
    params={"$expand": f"extensions($filter=id eq '{EXT_NAME}')"}
).json()

ext_data = user_with_ext.get("extensions", [{}])[0]
print("employeeId:", ext_data.get("employeeId"))
print("costCenter:", ext_data.get("costCenter"))

# Update an existing extension
requests.patch(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{USER_ID}/extensions/{EXT_NAME}",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={"clearanceLevel": "Secret", "reviewDate": "2026-07-01"}
)
Dynamic Group Rules on User Attributes

Dynamic groups (P1 required) automatically maintain their membership based on a filter expression evaluated against user attributes. This is the recommended way to manage license assignments and app scoping at scale — new employees get the right groups without manual intervention.

-- All Engineering members
(user.department -eq "Engineering") and (user.userType -eq "Member")

-- All US employees with a specific job title prefix
(user.usageLocation -eq "US") and (user.jobTitle -startsWith "Senior")

-- All guests from a specific partner domain
(user.userType -eq "Guest") and (user.mail -contains "@fabrikam.com")

-- All active users hired in the last 30 days
-- (requires employeeHireDate; needs P1 + Governance for date-based rules)
(user.employeeHireDate -le system.now -and user.accountEnabled -eq True)

-- Users with a specific extension attribute value
(user.extensionAttribute5 -eq "Contractor")
Creating a Dynamic Group via Graph

import requests

dynamic_group = {
    "displayName": "sg-engineering-members",
    "mailEnabled": False,
    "securityEnabled": True,
    "mailNickname": "sg-engineering-members",
    "groupTypes": ["DynamicMembership"],        # marks this as a dynamic group
    "membershipRule": "(user.department -eq "Engineering") and (user.userType -eq "Member")",
    "membershipRuleProcessingState": "On"
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=dynamic_group
)
print(resp.status_code, resp.json().get("id"))

# Check processing status (membership evaluation is asynchronous)
import time
GROUP_ID = resp.json()["id"]
for _ in range(12):   # poll up to 2 min
    time.sleep(10)
    g = requests.get(f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups/{GROUP_ID}",
                     headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
                     params={"$select": "membershipRuleProcessingState"}).json()
    state = g.get("membershipRuleProcessingState")
    print(f"  Processing state: {state}")
    if state == "Paused" or state == "On":
        break

Entra ID supports several authentication methods per user. Administrators can read and manage a user's registered methods — useful for helpdesk tooling, compliance audits, and automating SSPR registration reminders.
Reading a User's Registered Auth Methods

import requests

USER_ID = "<user-object-id>"

methods = requests.get(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{USER_ID}/authentication/methods",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"}
).json().get("value", [])

for m in methods:
    method_type = m["@odata.type"].split(".")[-1]
    print(f"  {method_type}")
    if "phoneNumber" in m:
        print(f"    Phone: {m['phoneNumber']}")
    if "emailAddress" in m:
        print(f"    Email: {m['emailAddress']}")

# Common @odata.type values:
# microsoftAuthenticatorAuthenticationMethod  — Authenticator app
# phoneAuthenticationMethod                   — SMS / voice call
# fido2AuthenticationMethod                   — FIDO2 security key
# passwordAuthenticationMethod                — username+password
# emailAuthenticationMethod                   — OTP to alternate email (SSPR)

import requests

# If a user's phone was compromised, remove the SMS method
USER_ID = "<user-object-id>"
PHONE_METHOD_ID = "<phone-auth-method-id>"   # from GET /authentication/methods

resp = requests.delete(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{USER_ID}/authentication/phoneMethods/{PHONE_METHOD_ID}",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"}
)
print("Removed phone method:", resp.status_code == 204)

# Also immediately revoke all sessions
requests.post(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{USER_ID}/revokeSignInSessions",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"}
)
print("Sessions revoked")

Entra ID records every sign-in and every administrative action on user objects. These logs are available via the Microsoft Graph Reporting API and can be exported to Azure Monitor Logs for long-term retention and SIEM integration.
Querying Sign-In Logs for a Specific User

import requests

USER_UPN = "alice@contoso.com"

# Requires AuditLog.Read.All permission
signins = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/signIns",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
    params={
        "$filter": f"userPrincipalName eq '{USER_UPN}' and createdDateTime ge 2026-06-01T00:00:00Z",
        "$select": "createdDateTime,appDisplayName,ipAddress,location,status,conditionalAccessStatus",
        "$top": 50,
        "$orderby": "createdDateTime desc"
    }
).json().get("value", [])

print(f"Last {len(signins)} sign-ins for {USER_UPN}:")
for s in signins[:10]:
    outcome = "OK" if s["status"]["errorCode"] == 0 else f"FAIL({s['status']['errorCode']})"
    city = s.get("location", {}).get("city", "?")
    print(f"  {s['createdDateTime'][:19]}  {s['appDisplayName']:<30} {city:<15} {outcome}")
Audit Log: Who Created or Modified a User

import requests

# Find all admin actions that touched a specific user object
USER_ID = "<user-object-id>"

audits = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/directoryAudits",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
    params={
        "$filter": f"targetResources/any(t: t/id eq '{USER_ID}')",
        "$select": "activityDateTime,activityDisplayName,initiatedBy,result",
        "$orderby": "activityDateTime desc",
        "$top": 25
    }
).json().get("value", [])

for a in audits:
    actor = a.get("initiatedBy", {}).get("user", {}).get("userPrincipalName", "system")
    print(f"{a['activityDateTime'][:19]}  {a['activityDisplayName']:<40} by {actor}  result={a['result']}")

Entra ID user objects represent identities that can sign in to Microsoft cloud services. Members are internal users created in (or synced into) the tenant; Guests are external B2B collaborators invited by email. The userType property controls their default directory permissions and licensing behaviour.
Create member users via Graph API, Azure CLI, or Entra admin center; set usageLocation before assigning licenses
Invite guests with the /invitations endpoint; they authenticate against their home IdP
Use the stable immutable id GUID as a key in code — UPNs can change
Disable accounts and call revokeSignInSessions on termination; delete to move to 30-day recycle bin
Find stale accounts by querying signInActivity.lastSignInDateTime (requires AuditLog.Read.All)
SSPR with on-premises writeback requires P1 and Entra Connect password writeback enabled
